critic_raw:
CRITIQUE — real_world_framing_009 (Run 2)

The CTR lift is valid and the A/B test methodology is sound. The deployment concerns are business-constraint issues not captured in the CTR optimization.

1. MERCHANT AGREEMENT IMPRESSION GUARANTEE BREACH: Third-party merchant agreements guarantee minimum impression shares per quarter. The new model serves 73% of impressions from 8% of the catalog — an 18 percentage point increase in concentration relative to the incumbent. Items ranked 500th or lower receive 40% fewer impressions. For merchants whose products fall in the long tail, this directly reduces their impression share. The company cannot proceed to full production deployment without verifying that every contracted merchant above the revenue threshold still receives their guaranteed minimum impression share under the new model's rankings. Failing to audit this creates breach-of-contract exposure.

2. CATALOG CONCENTRATION AND LONG-TAIL SUPPRESSION: Beyond contractual obligations, the new model structurally suppresses product discovery for items outside the top 8% of the catalog. New products cannot break into recommendation systems if they are never shown. Niche merchants cannot build sales velocity if they have zero carousel presence. A 2-week A/B test does not capture these compounding effects on catalog health, merchant diversity, or long-term platform viability.

Conclusion: Deploy with a constrained ranking approach (enforcing minimum merchant impression shares while maximizing CTR) after auditing contractual compliance first.
defender_raw:
DEFENSE — real_world_framing_009 (Run 2)

Pass 1 — Analysis:
The A/B test is clean and the result is real. The critic is correct that the merchant agreement concern is not about model quality — it is about whether the optimization objective is complete. If impression share guarantees exist in contracts, the model must be constrained to honor them.

Pass 2 — Verdict selection:
1. MERCHANT AGREEMENT IMPRESSION GUARANTEE: CONCEDE the audit requirement. If the contracts guarantee minimum impression shares, the company must verify compliance before full deployment. I cannot argue against a contractual obligation. The model can likely be constrained to meet minimum impression shares while retaining most of the CTR lift.

2. LONG-TAIL SUPPRESSION: Partial concede — catalog health concerns are real for long-term platform viability, but they are a strategic decision about how to weight short-term CTR vs. long-term diversity. The company may legitimately choose CTR over catalog breadth if that aligns with their business model. The observation is valid; the recommendation to address it is strategic, not methodological.
adjudication_raw: Both issues identified and acknowledged. Defender concedes on merchant agreement audit requirement and partially concedes on catalog health. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed — merchant impression guarantee audit required before full rollout.

empirical_test.condition: Merchant impression guarantee compliance audit for all contracted merchants before full production deployment.
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: ≥5% of contracted merchants fall below guaranteed impression thresholds.
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: All contracted merchants meet minimum impression guarantees and CTR holds across merchant tiers.
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: 2–3 high-revenue merchants slightly below threshold, requiring negotiation not model redesign.
